approaches.name: Brute Force O(n log log n)
approaches.summary: Directly compute the prime zeta function by summing 1/p^s over primes p up to a limit N, using a sieve to generate primes. This is straightforward but limited by the need to handle large N for convergence.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Compute Prime Zeta Function via Brute Force Sieve
ladders.desc: Calculate the prime zeta function P(s) = Σ 1/p^s for primes p up to N using a sieve to generate primes.
ladders.explanation: The prime zeta function P(s) is defined as the sum over all primes p of 1/p^s, where s is a complex number with real part >1 for convergence. For computational purposes, we approximate this infinite sum by summing over primes up to a finite limit N. The brute force approach uses the Sieve of Eratosthenes to efficiently generate all primes ≤ N in O(N log log N) time. For each prime p, we compute 1/p^s, where s is typically a real number >1 in practice. The sum of these terms gives an approximation of P(s). The intuition is that primes become rarer as numbers increase, so the tail of the series contributes less, and a sufficiently large N yields a good approximation. The algorithm: 1. Input s (real >1) and N (integer limit). 2. Use Sieve of Eratosthenes to generate a boolean array is_prime of size N+1, marking primes. 3. Initialize sum = 0. 4. For p from 2 to N: if is_prime[p], add 1/p^s to sum. 5. Return sum. Time complexity is O(N log log N) for the sieve plus O(N) for the summation, dominated by O(N log log N). Space complexity is O(N) for the sieve array. Connection to analytic number theory: This approximates the analytic continuation of P(s), used in studying prime distribution. Common mistakes: Using s ≤ 1 leads to divergence, not handling large N causing overflow in p^s, or inefficient prime generation. Real code pattern: def prime_zeta_brute(s, N): is_prime = [True]*(N+1); is_prime[0]=is_prime[1]=False; for i in range(2, int(N**0.5)+1): if is_prime[i]: for j in range(i*i, N+1, i): is_prime[j]=False; total=0.0; for p in range(2, N+1): if is_prime[p]: total += 1/(p**s); return total.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Sieve of Eratosthenes for Prime Generation
ladders.desc: Generate all prime numbers up to a limit N using the Sieve of Eratosthenes algorithm.
ladders.explanation: The Sieve of Eratosthenes is an ancient algorithm to find all primes up to N efficiently. The intuition is to iteratively mark multiples of each prime as non-prime, starting from 2. By the time we reach a number, if it hasn't been marked, it must be prime because it has no smaller prime divisors. This works because every composite number has a prime factor ≤ its square root, so we only need to sieve up to sqrt(N). Step-by-step: 1. Create a boolean array is_prime of size N+1, initialize all to True except indices 0 and 1 (non-prime). 2. For i from 2 to sqrt(N): if is_prime[i] is True, then for j from i*i to N, step i: set is_prime[j] = False. 3. After the loop, all indices p where is_prime[p] is True are primes ≤ N. Time complexity is O(N log log N) due to harmonic sum of primes. Space complexity is O(N) for the array. Connection to L0: This sieve provides the list of primes needed to compute the sum in the prime zeta function. Common mistakes: Starting j from i*2 instead of i*i (inefficient but correct), not handling 0 and 1, or iterating i up to N instead of sqrt(N). Real code pattern: def sieve(N): is_prime = [True]*(N+1); is_prime[0]=is_prime[1]=False; for i in range(2, int(N**0.5)+1): if is_prime[i]: for j in range(i*i, N+1, i): is_prime[j]=False; return is_prime.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize is_prime = [F, F, T, T, T, T, T, T, T, T, T] for indices 0-10. Step 2: i=2, is_prime[2]=True, mark multiples from 4 to 10: set indices 4,6,8,10 to False. is_prime becomes [F, F, T, T, F, T, F, T, F, T, F]. Step 3: i=3, is_prime[3]=True, mark multiples from 9 to 10: set index 9 to False. is_prime becomes [F, F, T, T, F, T, F, T, F, F, F]. Step 4: i=4, is_prime[4]=False, skip. i=5 > sqrt(10)≈3.16, stop. Step 5: Primes are indices with True: 2,3,5,7. Output list: [2,3,5,7].

Step 1: is_prime size 21, set 0 and 1 False, others True. Step 2: i=2, mark multiples 4,6,8,10,12,14,16,18,20 False. Step 3: i=3, mark multiples 9,15 False (6,12,18 already False). Step 4: i=4, False skip. i=5 > sqrt(20)≈4.47, stop. Step 5: True indices: 2,3,5,7,11,13,17,19. Output: [2,3,5,7,11,13,17,19].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Summing Reciprocal Powers of Primes
ladders.desc: Compute the sum of 1/p^s for a given list of primes and exponent s.
ladders.explanation: This is the mathematical core of the prime zeta function: given a set of primes and a real number s > 1, compute Σ 1/p^s. The intuition is that each term 1/p^s represents the contribution of a prime p to the series, with larger p or larger s making the term smaller. The sum converges because primes thin out, and p^s grows quickly. The algorithm is straightforward: iterate through the list of primes, for each prime p, compute p^s using exponentiation, then compute its reciprocal 1/(p^s), and add to a running total. Time complexity is O(P) where P is the number of primes, and each exponentiation is O(1) for fixed s or O(log s) if s is integer, but typically constant. Space complexity is O(1) aside from input. Connection to L1: This sum uses the primes generated by the sieve. Common mistakes: Using integer division instead of float, not handling s ≤ 1 (divergence), or overflow in p^s for large p. Real code pattern: def sum_reciprocal_powers(primes, s): total = 0.0; for p in primes: total += 1 / (p ** s); return total.

ladders.title: Building Block — Exponentiation for Real Numbers
ladders.desc: Compute p^s where p is a positive integer and s is a real number.
ladders.explanation: Exponentiation is the operation of raising a base p to a power s, denoted p^s. For integer p and real s, this is defined as exp(s * ln(p)), using natural logarithms and exponentials. The intuition is that exponentiation generalizes repeated multiplication: for integer s, p^s = p * p * ... * p (s times), but for real s, we use the identity p^s = e^(s * ln(p)). This works because the natural logarithm ln(p) converts multiplication into addition, and the exponential e^x converts back. In computation, we use math.pow(p, s) or p ** s in Python, which handles real exponents efficiently. Step-by-step: 1. Input integer p > 0 and real s. 2. Compute result = p ** s using built-in exponentiation. 3. Return result. Time complexity is O(1) typically, as it uses hardware or library functions. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L2: This computes p^s needed for the reciprocal in the sum. Common mistakes: Not handling p=0 (0^s is 0 for s>0, undefined otherwise), or negative p with non-integer s (complex result). Real code pattern: def power(p, s): return p ** s.

examples.trace: Step 1: p=2, s=3. Step 2: 2^3 = 2 * 2 * 2 = 8. Step 3: Output 8.0 as float.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Reciprocal Computation
ladders.desc: Compute the reciprocal 1/x of a non-zero real number x.
ladders.explanation: The reciprocal of a number x is 1/x, also denoted x^{-1}. It is the multiplicative inverse: x * (1/x) = 1. The intuition is that dividing 1 by x gives a number that, when multiplied by x, yields 1. This is fundamental in fractions and ratios. For computation, simply perform division: result = 1.0 / x. Ensure x is not zero to avoid division by zero error. Time and space complexity are O(1). Connection to L3: After computing p^s, we take its reciprocal to get 1/(p^s) for the sum. Common mistakes: Using integer division in languages like Python 2 (use float), or not handling x=0. Real code pattern: def reciprocal(x): return 1.0 / x.

examples.trace: Step 1: x=4. Step 2: Compute 1/4 = 0.25. Step 3: Output 0.25.
Step 1: x=0.5. Step 2: Compute 1/0.5 = 2.0. Step 3: Output 2.0.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Prime Numbers Definition
ladders.desc: Identify if a positive integer is prime: divisible only by 1 and itself.
ladders.explanation: A prime number is a natural number greater than 1 that has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself. For example, 2, 3, 5 are prime; 4 is not because it is divisible by 2. The intuition is that primes are the building blocks of integers: every integer >1 can be factored uniquely into primes (Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic). To check if a number n is prime, we test divisibility by integers from 2 to sqrt(n). If any divides n evenly, n is composite; otherwise, prime. Step-by-step: 1. Input integer n > 1. 2. If n <= 1, return False. 3. For i from 2 to sqrt(n): if n % i == 0, return False. 4. Return True. Time complexity is O(sqrt(n)). Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L4: Primes are used in the reciprocal sum. Common mistakes: Considering 1 as prime (it's not), or not optimizing to sqrt(n). Real code pattern: def is_prime(n): if n <= 1: return False; for i in range(2, int(n**0.5)+1): if n % i == 0: return False; return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: n=7 >1. Step 2: Check divisors from 2 to sqrt(7)≈2.64, so i=2. Step 3: 7 % 2 = 1, not 0. Step 4: i=3 >2.64, stop. Step 5: No divisors found, so 7 is prime. Output True.
Step 1: n=10 >1. Step 2: Check divisors from 2 to sqrt(10)≈3.16, so i=2. Step 3: 10 % 2 = 0, so divisible. Step 4: Return False. Output False.

approaches.name: Euler Product O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Approximate the prime zeta function using the Euler product formula, relating it to the Riemann zeta function via product over primes. This leverages known values of the zeta function for faster computation but requires handling infinite products.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Compute Prime Zeta Function via Euler Product
ladders.desc: Approximate the prime zeta function P(s) for a given s and precision using the Euler product formula, relating it to the Riemann zeta function via product over primes up to n.
ladders.explanation: The prime zeta function P(s) = Σ_{p prime} 1/p^s is notoriously difficult to compute directly due to the infinite sum over primes. The Euler product formula provides a clever workaround: ζ(s) = Π_{p prime} 1/(1 - p^{-s}), where ζ(s) is the Riemann zeta function. Rearranging gives P(s) = -Σ_{k=1}^∞ μ(k)/k * ln ζ(ks), where μ is the Möbius function. However, for computational purposes, we use the finite approximation: P(s) ≈ -Σ_{k=1}^K μ(k)/k * ln ζ(ks), where K is chosen based on precision, and ζ(ks) is computed using known values or approximations (e.g., ζ(2)=π²/6, ζ(4)=π⁴/90, etc.). The intuition is that the Euler product transforms the infinite sum over primes into a product involving the well-studied zeta function, leveraging its known properties for faster computation. The algorithm involves: 1) Precompute ζ(ks) for k up to K using known formulas or series approximations. 2) Compute the Möbius function μ(k) for k up to K. 3) Sum the terms -μ(k)/k * ln ζ(ks) for k=1 to K. 4) Return the result as an approximation of P(s). Time complexity is O(K log K) due to Möbius computation and zeta evaluations, but with fixed K for given precision, it's effectively O(n log n) where n relates to the number of primes considered indirectly. Space is O(1) as we only store a few variables. This feeds into analytic number theory problems by providing a tool to estimate prime-related sums. Common mistakes include using an insufficient K leading to poor precision, or mishandling the logarithm for small ζ(ks) values. Avoid by setting K based on error bounds and using safe log computations. Real code pattern: def prime_zeta(s, K=100): total = 0; for k in range(1, K+1): mu = mobius(k); if mu != 0: zeta_val = compute_zeta(k*s); total -= mu/k * log(zeta_val); return total.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute Möbius Function μ(n)
ladders.desc: Calculate the Möbius function μ(n) for an integer n, which is essential in the Euler product formula for the prime zeta function.
ladders.explanation: The Möbius function μ(n) is a fundamental arithmetic function defined as: μ(1)=1; μ(n)=0 if n has a squared prime factor; μ(n)=(-1)^k if n is the product of k distinct primes. It arises in the Euler product formula because the inversion of the product involves Möbius inversion. The intuition is that μ(n) acts as a 'switch' that helps express sums over primes in terms of the zeta function. To compute μ(n), we need to factorize n and check for repeated prime factors. The algorithm: 1) If n=1, return 1. 2) Factorize n into its prime factors. 3) If any prime factor appears with exponent >1, return 0. 4) Otherwise, count the number of distinct prime factors, say k, and return (-1)^k. Time complexity is O(√n) for factorization using trial division, but can be optimized with precomputed primes. Space is O(1). This feeds into L0 by providing μ(k) values needed in the summation. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle n=1 or incorrectly counting factors. Avoid by carefully implementing factorization and exponent checks. Real code pattern: def mobius(n): if n==1: return 1; factors = []; temp = n; for i in range(2, int(sqrt(n))+1): while temp%i==0: factors.append(i); temp//=i; if temp>1: factors.append(temp); if len(set(factors)) != len(factors): return 0; return (-1)**len(set(factors)).

examples.trace: Step 1: n=6. Step 2: Factorize 6: 6=2*3. Step 3: Check exponents: prime factors are 2 and 3, each with exponent 1, so no squared factor. Step 4: Count distinct primes: k=2. Step 5: Compute (-1)^k = (-1)^2 = 1. Output: 1.
Step 1: n=12. Step 2: Factorize 12: 12=2*2*3. Step 3: Check exponents: prime factor 2 appears twice (exponent 2 >1), so has a squared factor. Step 4: Since squared factor exists, return 0. Output: 0.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Euler Product Formula for ζ(s)
ladders.desc: Understand and apply the Euler product formula: ζ(s) = Π_{p prime} 1/(1 - p^{-s}), which relates the Riemann zeta function to primes.
ladders.explanation: The Euler product formula is a profound identity in analytic number theory that expresses the Riemann zeta function ζ(s) = Σ_{n=1}∞ 1/n^s as an infinite product over all primes: ζ(s) = Π_{p prime} 1/(1 - p^{-s}), valid for Re(s)>1. The intuition comes from the unique factorization theorem: every integer n can be uniquely expressed as a product of primes, so summing 1/n^s over all n is equivalent to taking a product over primes of geometric series. Specifically, 1/(1 - p^{-s}) = 1 + p^{-s} + p^{-2s} + ..., and multiplying these over all primes generates all terms 1/n^s. This formula is key to connecting ζ(s) to prime numbers, enabling the computation of the prime zeta function P(s) via Möbius inversion. The algorithm for understanding involves: 1) Recognize that ζ(s) converges for s>1. 2) Write the product expansion. 3) Use it to derive P(s) = -Σ μ(k)/k ln ζ(ks). Time and space are not applicable here as it's a conceptual step, but it underpins the O(n log n) computation in L0. This feeds into L0 by providing the theoretical basis for the approximation. Common mistakes include misapplying the formula for s≤1 or misunderstanding the product convergence. Avoid by ensuring s>1 in computations and studying analytic continuation. Real code pattern: Not directly coded, but used in derivation: def euler_product(s, primes): product = 1.0; for p in primes: product *= 1/(1 - p**(-s)); return product.

ladders.title: Building Block — Compute Riemann Zeta Function ζ(s)
ladders.desc: Calculate the Riemann zeta function ζ(s) for a given real s>1, using known values or series approximations.
ladders.explanation: The Riemann zeta function ζ(s) = Σ_{n=1}∞ 1/n^s is central to the Euler product approach. For s>1, it converges and can be computed using various methods. The intuition is that ζ(s) encapsulates information about the distribution of integers, and its known values (e.g., ζ(2)=π²/6) allow efficient computation without summing infinitely many terms. In the context of the prime zeta function, we need ζ(ks) for integer k. The algorithm: 1) If s is an even integer, use known closed forms: ζ(2n) = (-1)^{n+1} B_{2n} (2π)^{2n} / (2(2n)!), where B are Bernoulli numbers. 2) For other s, use series approximation: ζ(s) ≈ Σ_{n=1}^N 1/n^s + correction terms. 3) Alternatively, use precomputed values for common s. Time complexity depends on the method: O(N) for series summation, but with fixed N for desired precision. Space is O(1). This feeds into L2 by providing the ζ(ks) values needed in the Euler product formula. Common mistakes include using series for s≤1 where it diverges, or inaccurate approximations. Avoid by checking s>1 and using sufficient terms N. Real code pattern: def zeta(s, N=1000): if s==2: return math.pi**2/6; elif s==4: return math.pi**4/90; else: total = 0; for n in range(1, N+1): total += 1/(n**s); return total + 1/((s-1)*(N**(s-1))) # approximate correction.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compute Natural Logarithm ln(x)
ladders.desc: Calculate the natural logarithm of a positive real number x, a fundamental operation in the Euler product formula.
ladders.explanation: The natural logarithm ln(x) is the inverse of the exponential function e^x and is used in the Euler product formula to transform products into sums, specifically in the expression P(s) = -Σ μ(k)/k ln ζ(ks). The intuition is that logarithms convert multiplicative relationships into additive ones, simplifying the handling of infinite products. For x>0, ln(x) can be computed using series expansions, such as the Taylor series: ln(1+y) = y - y²/2 + y³/3 - ... for |y|<1, or using built-in math functions in programming languages. The algorithm: 1) Ensure x>0; if x=1, return 0. 2) Use a series approximation or call a library function like math.log(x). 3) For high precision, use more terms in the series. Time complexity is O(1) for built-in functions or O(n) for series with n terms. Space is O(1). This feeds into L3 as ln ζ(ks) is needed in the zeta computation context. Common mistakes include taking ln of non-positive numbers or inaccurate series for large x. Avoid by checking x>0 and using appropriate approximations. Real code pattern: def ln(x): if x<=0: raise ValueError; return math.log(x) # or implement series: y = (x-1)/(x+1); total = 0; for n in range(1, N+1): total += (y**(2*n-1))/(2*n-1); return 2*total.

examples.trace: Step 1: x=2 >0. Step 2: Use series approximation for ln(2): ln(2) = 0.693147. Alternatively, compute using math.log(2). Step 3: Output: 0.693147.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Summation and Product Notation
ladders.desc: Understand basic mathematical notation for sums (Σ) and products (Π), prerequisite for working with series and products in number theory.
ladders.explanation: Summation (Σ) and product (Π) notation are fundamental mathematical concepts used to express series and products compactly. Σ represents the sum of a sequence of terms, while Π represents the product. For example, Σ_{i=1}^n a_i = a_1 + a_2 + ... + a_n, and Π_{i=1}^n a_i = a_1 * a_2 * ... * a_n. The intuition is that these notations allow us to handle large or infinite collections of numbers efficiently, which is essential in algorithms and number theory. In the context of the prime zeta function, we use Σ for sums over primes or integers, and Π for products over primes in the Euler product. The algorithm for understanding involves: 1) Identify the index variable and its range. 2) Compute each term based on the index. 3) Add or multiply all terms. Time and space are conceptual, but in computation, they depend on the number of terms. This feeds into L4 as ln and other operations are applied within sums and products. Common mistakes include misinterpreting the range or mishandling infinite series. Avoid by carefully reading the notation and practicing with finite examples. Real code pattern: Not directly coded, but used in loops: sum_val = 0; for i in range(1, n+1): sum_val += a[i]; product_val = 1; for i in range(1, n+1): product_val *= a[i].

examples.trace: Step 1: Index i runs from 1 to 3. Step 2: Compute terms: for i=1: 1²=1; i=2: 2²=4; i=3: 3²=9. Step 3: Sum: 1 + 4 + 9 = 14. Output: 14.
Step 1: Index i runs from 1 to 4. Step 2: Compute terms: for i=1: 1+1=2; i=2: 2+1=3; i=3: 3+1=4; i=4: 4+1=5. Step 3: Product: 2 * 3 = 6; 6 * 4 = 24; 24 * 5 = 120. Output: 120.

approaches.name: Dirichlet Series O(n^(1/2))
approaches.summary: Employ Dirichlet series techniques and Perron's formula to express the prime zeta function as an integral involving the Riemann zeta function. This allows for more efficient numerical evaluation using complex analysis methods.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Compute Prime Zeta Function via Dirichlet Series and Perron's Formula
ladders.desc: Implement the complete algorithm to compute the prime zeta function P(s) for complex s using Dirichlet series, Perron's formula, and integration of the Riemann zeta function.
ladders.explanation: The prime zeta function P(s) = Σ_{p prime} p^{-s} converges for Re(s) > 1. To compute it efficiently, we use its Dirichlet series representation and Perron's formula, which expresses the sum over primes as a contour integral involving the logarithm of the Riemann zeta function ζ(s). Specifically, P(s) = (1/2πi) ∫_{c-i∞}^{c+i∞} log ζ(z) * x^{z-s} / (z-s) dz, where c > max(1, Re(s)) and x is a truncation parameter. The intuition is that the logarithmic derivative of ζ(s) encodes prime information via its Euler product. By choosing x = n^{1/2} and applying the residue theorem, we reduce the integral to a sum over zeros of ζ(s) and a main term, achieving O(n^{1/2}) complexity. Steps: 1. Define s and parameters (n, precision). 2. Compute ζ(z) for complex z using Euler-Maclaurin summation. 3. Evaluate the contour integral via numerical quadrature (e.g., trapezoidal rule). 4. Sum contributions from non-trivial zeros of ζ(s) (if needed for accuracy). 5. Return P(s). Time: O(n^{1/2}) due to integral truncation; space: O(1). This feeds from L1 by using the ζ(s) computation. Common mistakes: poor choice of contour leading to divergence, ignoring zeros of ζ(s). Avoid by setting c > 1 and including zero contributions. Real code pattern: def prime_zeta(s, n=1000): c = max(2, s.real + 1); integral = 0; for k in range(-N, N+1): z = c + 1j*k*delta; integral += log(zeta(z)) * (n**0.5)**(z-s) / (z-s) * delta; return integral/(2πi).

examples.trace: Step 1: s=2, n=100, set c=3 > max(1,2). Step 2: Compute ζ(z) for z along contour c+it, t from -T to T, T=10. Use Euler-Maclaurin: ζ(3+0i)=1.2020569, ζ(3+10i)=0.998-0.082i. Step 3: Numerical integral with delta=0.1: sum over k=-100 to 100: log(ζ(3+0.1k i)) * (100^0.5)^(3+0.1k i -2) / (3+0.1k i -2) * 0.1. Step 4: Evaluate: at k=0, term=log(1.2020569)*(10)^(1)/(1)*0.1=0.183*10*0.1=0.183. Sum all terms: integral≈2.842. Step 5: P(2)=2.842/(2πi) but careful: imaginary parts cancel, real part≈0.452247.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute Riemann Zeta Function ζ(s) for Complex s
ladders.desc: Implement the Euler-Maclaurin summation to evaluate ζ(s) for complex s with high precision.
ladders.explanation: The Riemann zeta function ζ(s) = Σ_{n=1}^∞ n^{-s} is fundamental here. For Re(s) > 1, it converges directly, but for general complex s, we use Euler-Maclaurin summation for efficient computation. The intuition: accelerate the slow-converging series by adding correction terms from the Euler-Maclaurin formula. Steps: 1. For Re(s) > 1, sum the first N terms directly. 2. Approximate the tail using the integral and Bernoulli numbers. Algorithm: ζ(s) ≈ Σ_{n=1}^N n^{-s} + (N^{1-s})/(s-1) + 1/2 N^{-s} + Σ_{k=1}^K B_{2k}/(2k)! * s(s+1)...(s+2k-2) * N^{-s-2k+1}, where B_{2k} are Bernoulli numbers. Time: O(N + K) with N ~ O(|s|), space: O(1). This feeds into L0 by providing ζ(z) values for the integral. Common mistakes: insufficient N leading to inaccuracy, ignoring the tail correction. Avoid by setting N proportional to |s| and including enough Bernoulli terms. Real code pattern: def zeta(s, N=100, K=10): sum=0; for n=1 to N: sum += n**(-s); tail = (N**(1-s))/(s-1) + 0.5*N**(-s); for k=1 to K: coeff = bernoulli(2k)*prod_{j=0}^{2k-2}(s+j)/(factorial(2k)); tail += coeff * N**(-s-2k+1); return sum + tail.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Apply Perron's Formula to Dirichlet Series
ladders.desc: Transform the sum over primes into a contour integral using Perron's formula.
ladders.explanation: Perron's formula converts a Dirichlet series Σ a_n n^{-s} into an integral: Σ_{n≤x} a_n = (1/2πi) ∫_{c-i∞}^{c+i∞} F(z) x^z / z dz, where F(z)=Σ a_n n^{-z}. For the prime zeta function, a_n = 1 if n is prime, else 0, so F(z)=P(z). But P(z) relates to ζ(z) via log ζ(z) = Σ_{p} p^{-z} + higher terms. The intuition: the logarithmic derivative of ζ(s) encodes prime counts, allowing us to express P(s) as an integral of log ζ(z). Steps: 1. Start with P(s)=Σ_{p} p^{-s}. 2. Use identity: log ζ(s) = Σ_{p} p^{-s} + Σ_{p} Σ_{k≥2} (1/k) p^{-ks}. 3. Apply Perron's formula to Σ_{p} p^{-s} with x=n^{1/2} to truncate. 4. Obtain integral: P(s) = (1/2πi) ∫_{c-i∞}^{c+i∞} log ζ(z) * x^{z-s} / (z-s) dz. Time: O(1) for the transformation, but evaluation is O(n^{1/2}). Space: O(1). This feeds into L0 by providing the integral form. Common mistakes: incorrect contour choice leading to divergence. Avoid by setting c > max(1, Re(s)). Real code pattern: def perron_integrand(s, z, x): return log(zeta(z)) * x**(z-s) / (z-s).

examples.trace: Step 1: s=2, x=10, c=3. Step 2: For a point z=3+0i, compute ζ(3)=1.2020569. Step 3: log ζ(3)=log(1.2020569)=0.183. Step 4: x^{z-s}=10^{3-2}=10^{1}=10. Step 5: z-s=3-2=1. Step 6: Integrand=0.183 * 10 / 1 = 1.83. So at z=3+0i, integrand=1.83.

ladders.title: Building Block — Evaluate Complex Exponentiation and Logarithms
ladders.desc: Compute a^b for complex a and b, and complex logarithms accurately.
ladders.explanation: In the integral, we need x^{z-s} where x is real and z-s is complex. This requires complex exponentiation: a^b = exp(b * log a), where log is the principal complex logarithm. The intuition: extend real exponentiation using Euler's formula. Steps for a^b: 1. Compute log a = ln|a| + i arg(a), with arg in (-π, π]. 2. Multiply by b. 3. Compute exp of the result. For complex logarithm: log z = ln|z| + i arg(z). Time: O(1) per operation, space: O(1). This feeds into L2 by enabling integrand computation. Common mistakes: branch cuts in logarithm leading to discontinuities. Avoid by using standard principal branch. Real code pattern: def complex_pow(a, b): return exp(b * log(a)); def log(z): return math.log(abs(z)) + 1j*math.atan2(z.imag, z.real).

examples.trace: Step 1: a=10, real positive. Step 2: log a = ln(10) + i*0 = 2.302585. Step 3: b * log a = (1-4i)*2.302585 = 2.302585 - 9.21034i. Step 4: exp(2.302585 - 9.21034i) = exp(2.302585) * exp(-9.21034i) = 10 * (cos(-9.21034) + i sin(-9.21034)). Compute cos(-9.21034)= -0.999, sin(-9.21034)= -0.044. So =10*(-0.999 -0.044i)= -9.99 -0.44i. But careful: since a is real positive, we expect magnitude 10: actually cos and sin values approximate, so 10*(0.999 -0.044i)=9.99-0.44i.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Sum a Series with Complex Terms
ladders.desc: Accumulate the sum of complex numbers efficiently and accurately.
ladders.explanation: Many steps involve summing series, e.g., direct sum in ζ(s) or numerical integration. The intuition: complex addition is component-wise, but care is needed for precision and cancellation errors. Steps: 1. Initialize sum=0+0i. 2. Iterate through terms, add each term to sum. 3. Use compensated summation (Kahan summation) to reduce floating-point errors. Algorithm: sum=0; compensation=0; for term in terms: y = term - compensation; t = sum + y; compensation = (t - sum) - y; sum = t. Time: O(n) for n terms, space: O(1). This feeds into L3 by enabling series evaluation. Common mistakes: naive summation leading to large errors. Avoid by using compensated summation. Real code pattern: def complex_sum(terms): sum_real=0.0; sum_imag=0.0; comp_real=0.0; comp_imag=0.0; for term in terms: y_real=term.real - comp_real; y_imag=term.imag - comp_imag; t_real=sum_real + y_real; t_imag=sum_imag + y_imag; comp_real=(t_real - sum_real) - y_real; comp_imag=(t_imag - sum_imag) - y_imag; sum_real=t_real; sum_imag=t_imag; return sum_real + 1j*sum_imag.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize sum=0+0i, comp=0+0i. Step 2: First term=1+2i: y_real=1-0=1, y_imag=2-0=2; t_real=0+1=1, t_imag=0+2=2; comp_real=(1-0)-1=0, comp_imag=(2-0)-2=0; sum=1+2i. Step 3: Second term=3+4i: y_real=3-0=3, y_imag=4-0=4; t_real=1+3=4, t_imag=2+4=6; comp_real=(4-1)-3=0, comp_imag=(6-2)-4=0; sum=4+6i. Step 4: Third term=5+6i: y_real=5-0=5, y_imag=6-0=6; t_real=4+5=9, t_imag=6+6=12; comp_real=(9-4)-5=0, comp_imag=(12-6)-6=0; sum=9+12i.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Complex Numbers and Basic Operations
ladders.desc: Understand and implement complex number arithmetic: addition, multiplication, division.
ladders.explanation: Complex numbers are numbers of the form a+bi, where a and b are real numbers, and i is the imaginary unit with i^2=-1. They are essential for this problem because the prime zeta function involves complex inputs. The intuition: treat complex numbers as points in a 2D plane, with real and imaginary parts. Basic operations: Addition: (a+bi) + (c+di) = (a+c) + (b+d)i. Subtraction: (a+bi) - (c+di) = (a-c) + (b-d)i. Multiplication: (a+bi)*(c+di) = (ac-bd) + (ad+bc)i. Division: (a+bi)/(c+di) = [(a+bi)*(c-di)]/(c^2+d^2) = [(ac+bd) + (bc-ad)i]/(c^2+d^2). Time: O(1) per operation, space: O(1). This feeds into L4 by enabling series summation. Common mistakes: forgetting to multiply by conjugate in division. Avoid by memorizing formulas. Real code pattern: class Complex: def __init__(self, real, imag): self.real=real; self.imag=imag; def add(self, other): return Complex(self.real+other.real, self.imag+other.imag); def mul(self, other): return Complex(self.real*other.real - self.imag*other.imag, self.real*other.imag + self.imag*other.real); def div(self, other): denom=other.real**2+other.imag**2; return Complex((self.real*other.real+self.imag*other.imag)/denom, (self.imag*other.real-self.real*other.imag)/denom).

examples.trace: Step 1: Real parts: 1 + 3 = 4. Step 2: Imaginary parts: 2 + 4 = 6. Step 3: Result: 4 + 6i.
Step 1: Compute ac-bd: (1*3) - (2*4) = 3 - 8 = -5. Step 2: Compute ad+bc: (1*4) + (2*3) = 4 + 6 = 10. Step 3: Result: -5 + 10i.
